content.content:
1	# Bedrock Quire — Consume-Side Shaping
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-09
4	
5	Status: intent/shaping. Nothing here is built. This memo records the *shape* of a
6	future Recipe Bottle subsystem for safely consuming frontier models on AWS Bedrock,
7	so the durable conclusions survive into the roadmap and a later build. It supersedes
8	nothing; it **extends** `memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (which predates
9	the identity model and the consume/host split) and **rides** the citizen/capability
10	identity model of heat ₣BZ (`rbk-14-mvp-citizen-model`).
11	
12	This is a *successor* to the 2026-04-15 conduit memo, not an edit of it — that memo is a
13	dated snapshot and keeps its provenance. Where the two disagree, this one is current.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## 1. Scope — consume, not host
18	
19	The need is **safe, private use of frontier models** (Claude and peers on Bedrock) for the
20	operator and the operator's users. Conversations must stay private. That is the whole point.
21	
22	Explicitly **in scope**: invoking *native foundation models already on Bedrock* from a
23	contained client.
24	
25	Explicitly **out of scope** (collapses away because we are not hosting our own weights):
26	the embassy / bullion / entrust / Custom-Model-Import delivery path. We are not pushing
27	models to AWS. The roadmap's `chantry` entry imagined both a custom-imported model and a
28	native one; only the native-consume half lives here.
29	
30	A consequence worth stating plainly up front (it corrects the operator's initial mental
31	model): **Bedrock is not an "instance" you run and keep secure.** It is a fully managed,
32	serverless, *stateless* request/response API. There is no box to harden, and — per Recipe
33	Bottle's **asymmetric-sovereignty** stance — no AWS principal Recipe Bottle owns. What you
34	control is: which model you invoke, the IAM perimeter, the network path, the logging switch,
35	and the AWS account itself. "Secure within Recipe Bottle" therefore splits: the *consuming*
36	end is secured inside a bottle; the *serving* end is yours in AWS, stood up from
37	Recipe-Bottle-shipped templates but owned by you wearing an AWS hat.
38	
39	## 2. Why Recipe Bottle holds the persistent state
40	
41	Bedrock is stateless: each call is independent, and even the conversation history is held by
42	the *client* and re-sent every turn. So the durable truth of "which model, reached how, under
43	whose identity, at what cost ceiling" has **nowhere to live on the Bedrock side.** That is the
44	argument for a persistent Recipe Bottle config model: statelessness over there forces
45	persistence over here.
46	
47	## 3. The privacy model — six boundaries, one Palisade
48	
49	"Completely private" decomposes into distinct boundaries with distinct strengths. The first
50	four can be driven to near-airtight; the last two are the honest ceiling.
51	
52	- **A — From the public internet (network).** PrivateLink keeps traffic on the AWS network;
53	  allowlist + TLS is the public-path fallback. Strong; delivered client-side by the conduit.
54	- **B — From the model provider.** Architectural: Bedrock runs each provider's model in an
55	  isolated deployment account the provider cannot reach; zero provider operator access. Under
56	  Commercial Terms (which Bedrock is), the provider does not train on your data. Strong.
57	- **C — From your own logs.** Bedrock defaults to zero-data-retention; model-invocation
58	  logging is opt-in, off by default. The main self-inflicted leak is turning logging on and
59	  under-securing the sink. Default: leave it off.
60	- **D — From AWS retention/training/sharing.** Contractual + ZDR; abuse detection is fully
61	  automated, no human review, no storage (verify per model — a few carry a 30-day
62	  classifier-flagged exception; Claude is in the clean automated-only camp).
63	- **E — From AWS hardware at the moment of inference.** The boundary **no hosted frontier
64	  model can cross.** The model must read your plaintext to answer; AWS silicon processes
65	  cleartext. The guarantee is *contractual + architectural + compliance-audited* — genuinely
66	  strong, the same class of trust under all enterprise cloud — but trust *placed*, not trust
67	  *eliminated*. If the threat model requires "the operator mathematically cannot see it,"
68	  nothing hosted meets that.
69	- **F — From server-side agency egress (NEW).** Plain inference (`Converse`/`InvokeModel`)
70	  has *zero* network reach — the model is a pure prompt→completion function and even a tool
71	  *decision* never touches the wire. But agentic surfaces — Bedrock Agents with Lambda action
72	  groups, and AgentCore Browser / Code Interpreter / the server-side tool execution of the
73	  Responses API — execute **server-side** and can reach the open internet **behind the
74	  Palisade, entirely outside the sentry's view.** This is opt-in (a different, more complex
75	  API surface; you never get it by accident), and the **only** lever over it is **IAM**, not
76	  the sentry.
77	
78	**The Palisade.** Boundaries A, C, and the client side of B/D sit inside our realm and get
79	full sovereignty. E is irreducible foreign ground — characterize and contain, do not pretend
80	to govern. F is the subtle one: the sentry governs the *bottle's* egress and is structurally
81	blind to *Bedrock's* server-side agency, so F is governed only by IAM and by *not invoking
82	agentic surfaces*. The private Quire uses non-agentic surfaces only; agentic Bedrock is a
83	deliberate, named re-opening of the egress question on AWS's side of the Pale.
84	
85	## 4. What Recipe Bottle does — and does not — do
86	
87	Recipe Bottle **establishes and maintains the binding**: the config, the identity, the reach,
88	the cost envelope. The **inference calls are made by whatever runs inside the bottle** (an
89	app, a chat client, an orchestrator) — *not* by Recipe Bottle. There is therefore **no
90	"submit" verb**. Recipe Bottle's surface ends at "the path and identity are ready and
91	verified." The only place Recipe Bottle itself touches Bedrock is a one-shot *probe* (does it
92	answer?), a health check, not a workload call.
93	
94	This single rule shapes the whole vocabulary: the verbs are *lifecycle of the binding*, never
95	invocation.
96	
97	## 5. Vocabulary
98	
99	The roadmap's working words `chantry` (target) and `conduit` (reach) both start with `C`,
100	which stumbles against Crucible. Renamed to unbound primary letters:
101	
102	| Concept | Noun | Verbs | Register | Cardinality |
103	|---|---|---|---|---|
104	| The model endpoint a bottle consumes | **Quire** | `endow` / `dissolve` | ecclesiastical (heir to *chantry*'s "where the voice issues") | 0..N |
105	| The peer-bearing private reach into an AWS VPC | **Outpost** | `raise` / `strike` | camp/diplomatic (sibling to *embassy*) | 0..1 |
106	
107	All four verbs are collision-free across RB and JJK today (`grep` lands clean — the
108	findability rationale the operator asked us to favor).
109	
110	**Two tiers of lifecycle.** The Outpost is the heavy, shared, singleton reach (raise once,
111	strike once). Quires are the light bindings that ride it (endow/dissolve freely). This is the
112	conduit-vs-target split, resolved: the reach object is first-class, not footprint — its 0..1
113	cardinality is exactly what earns it a noun and its own verbs.
114	
115	**The peer is *not* separately named.** It is the Outpost's compute; `raise`/`strike` operate
116	on the Outpost, which includes the peer. No peer-lifecycle verbs — the peer is hourly-cheap,
117	stop/start saves only noise, and the endpoint/EIP are standing anyway.
118	
119	**Prefix allocation (open minting item).** A Quire regime is a natural sibling to
120	nameplate/vessel; `RBRQ` is a free slot in the `RBR*` family. The Outpost cannot be `RBRO`
121	(taken by the OAuth regime), so its config home needs a non-`RBRO` slot — deferred to the
122	later minting pass.
123	
124	## 6. Config shape — three orthogonal layers
125	
126	A "Bedrock configuration" is not one blob; it is three independently-variable layers (same
127	model reachable two ways; same reach under two identities; same identity to three models).
128	They want composable homes, not a monolith.
129	
130	- **Target — the Quire.** Fields: model(s); version-pin (Bedrock model IDs drift — pin for
131	  provenance, as we pin everything else); region + inference profile (single-region or
132	  geographic, **not global** by default — a silent residency/privacy knob); **`agency`** (see
133	  below); a capability reference (who may invoke); a reach selector; logging posture
134	  (`off` by default).
135	- **Reach — `allowlist` or `outpost`.** This extends the nameplate's network posture. The
136	  MVP value is `allowlist` with **no Outpost in existence**; the fast-follow raises an Outpost
137	  and repoints. Egress allowlist scope for a private Quire: the Bedrock endpoint, plus the STS
138	  endpoint if federating, and **nothing else** — the egress-lockdown crown jewel.
139	- **Identity — the invoking principal.** MVP: a confined credential placed in the bottle
140	  (RBRA-style regime), because ₣BZ's federation is future. Fast-follow: federated short-lived
141	  STS (the envoy pattern), no resting key. Either way the identity is decoupled from
142	  capability per ₣BZ.
143	
144	**The `agency` field (boundary F's declared home).** A lattice, not a boolean:
145	
146	- `none` — plain inference, no server-side tools. (privacy-max; the MVP default and the only
147	  MVP value)
148	- `internal` — server-side tools, but only your resources (e.g. a knowledge base over your
149	  data); no internet.
150	- `internet` — server-side tools that reach the open web (AgentCore Browser, etc.).
151	  (privacy-off)
152	
153	`agency` is *declared intent that IAM enforces* — the same intent-vs-enforcement spine as
154	₣BZ. The Quire's invocation principal carries an agency clause; an audit can diff declared vs.
155	actual IAM. Even though every MVP Quire is `agency: none`, declaring it earns its keep: it
156	makes the privacy guarantee an explicit, enforced property rather than an absence, and gives
157	the IAM *denial* a documented home.
158	
159	**Note the two outward directions, kept distinct.** `reach` is how the *bottle* gets *in* to
160	Bedrock (sentry-governed). `agency` is how far the *model* may act *out* (IAM-governed). Same
161	Quire, opposite directions, one field each — do not let one blur into the other.
162	
163	## 7. Staging
164	
165	**MVP** — allowlist Quire, single Quire, `agency: none`, plain inference, confined credential
166	in the bottle, no Outpost, no VPC, no standing AWS infrastructure. This already delivers the
167	privacy story: boundaries A (TLS + egress-lockdown), B, C, D hold; the bottle excludes the
168	workstation from the data path and can leak nowhere but Bedrock. **The VPC is not what keeps
169	conversations private — this MVP already does.**
170	
171	**Fast-follow** — raise an Outpost (PrivateLink), repoint Quires to `reach: outpost`, and
172	upgrade identity to federation. Build the VPC only when one of three triggers fires:
173	1. egress **exclusivity** is required — allowlisting Bedrock by static CIDR is ≈ "all of
174	   Amazon" (no dedicated published range); domain/SNI is tighter but shares IPs; PrivateLink
175	   is the one *exclusive*, stable-CIDR answer. **This is the VPC's one non-premature job** —
176	   exclusivity, distinct from the privacy boundaries A–D, which the MVP already covers.
177	2. you need to hide network metadata (that you talk to Bedrock at all), or
178	3. you want private paths to *several* AWS services and want to amortize the peer.
179	
180	Until then the Outpost is premature — and it spends the "no instance to secure" win, since a
181	private tunnel into a VPC requires a standing termination point (the peer or a pricier managed
182	VPN; there is no serverless inbound tunnel).
183	
184	## 8. Cost
185	
186	The Outpost's *always-on* floor (accrues whether or not anyone infers):
187	
188	| Component | ~Monthly |
189	|---|---|
190	| EC2 `t3.nano` peer | $3.80 |
191	| Elastic IP (1 public IPv4) | $3.65 (possibly ~$0 under the 750-hr in-use allowance) |
192	| PrivateLink interface endpoint (1 AZ) | $7.30 |
193	| Data processing (text payloads) | pennies |
194	| VPC / subnets / route tables / security groups | free |
195	| **Total (single-AZ)** | **≈ $15** |
196	
197	Dual-AZ for High Availability (HA = a redundant copy in a second Availability Zone, so one
198	datacenter outage doesn't kill the path) adds ~$7/mo. **Skip HA** for a solo/small private
199	path; reconnect when the AZ recovers.
200	
201	The **one** cost to actively avoid is a **NAT gateway** (~$32/mo + data). The design routes
202	through the peer and never needs one; keep it out of the provisioning templates. (A NAT
203	gateway is for private subnets reaching the *public* internet — which this design never does.)
204	
205	Comparison: Claude on Bedrock is Sonnet **$3/$15** and Opus **$5/$25** per million in/out
206	tokens (+~10% cross-region; caching up to 90% off cached input). The entire ~$15/mo floor ≈ 1
207	million Sonnet output tokens ≈ **one active user's day or two** of real work. At any genuine
208	usage the conduit hosting is rounding error. **Optimize the Outpost for correctness and
209	security, not for its hosting cost.**
210	
211	## 9. Horizon — JJK-choreographed multi-Quire interactions
212	
213	This is the forward vision that shapes the present design without being built here.
214	
215	Future Job-Jockey-choreographed LLM interactions enlist *several Quires of different natures*
216	— different models, different `agency` levels, different residency — across one tightly
217	managed conversation. A private-counsel Quire (Opus, `agency: none`), a research Quire
218	(`agency: internet`), a fast-triage Quire (Haiku, `none`); an orchestrator routes among them.
219	This is the argument for a Quire being a **typed, governed object**, not a thin model pointer.
220	
221	**"A Quire identity enables this," grounded.** A Quire's identity is, concretely, its
222	**governed invocation principal** — the IAM role/policy that encodes its nature (model access,
223	agency ceiling, residency, capability). Enlisting a Quire = using its principal. The identity
224	is not person-like; it is a *named policy bundle* that makes Quires addressable and governable
225	in a choreography rather than merely callable.
226	
227	**Instance and menu, resolved by altitude (settled 2026-06-10).** A Quire is the
228	**endpoint-side envelope**: what exists to be invoked — approved models, reach, residency,
229	logging posture. The menu-ness lives in the **prebend** (§12), the **citizen-side envelope**:
230	who may invoke, at what agency ceiling, under what allowance. A concrete invocation must fall
231	within the **intersection of the two envelopes** at use-time — two lattices meeting where the
232	call happens. This dissolves the either/or: the Quire may be as instance-like as the AWS
233	resources beneath it, because the subselection rights migrated to the prebend. The division
234	of labor stands as stated: **Recipe Bottle governs the envelopes — approvals, allowances,
235	availability, cost management — and the consumer picks within their intersection.** This also
236	strengthens §2 — what Recipe Bottle persists is precisely the two envelopes (the governed,
237	stateless-side-absent state), and the concrete invocation stays ephemeral, mirroring
238	Bedrock's own statelessness.
239	
240	**Privacy becomes a graph property.** The deepest consequence: in a multi-Quire choreography,
241	confidentiality no longer lives in the nodes. If a private Quire's output (`agency: none`)
242	flows into an agentic Quire's input (`agency: internet`), the private data can exfiltrate
243	**through the sibling's agency** — and no per-Quire guarantee catches it, because each Quire
244	is individually well-behaved. So "tightly managed conversations" must carry **information-flow
245	rules** — which Quire's output may feed which Quire's input — and the confidentiality lives in
246	the *edges* of the conversation graph.
247	
248	Elegantly, the `agency` lattice (`none < internal < internet`) **doubles as the clearance
249	lattice** for that flow control: *output from a lower-agency Quire may not flow into a
250	higher-agency Quire without an explicit declassification step.* The field added for the MVP is
251	the same primitive the future choreography needs — a sign the shape is right.
252	
253	The orchestration itself is a separate future effort (JJK-adjacent) and must not touch the
254	MVP. Recorded here as a shaping force and one named hazard, nothing more.
255	
256	## 10. Open / deferred
257	
258	- **Prefix minting** — `RBRQ` candidate for the Quire regime; Outpost needs a non-`RBRO` slot
259	  (RBRO taken by OAuth). The §12 governance nouns (Precentor, Chorister, Prebend, Cloister)
260	  join the same queue, as does the unnamed **probe verb** — RB's one Quire-touching call
261	  (register candidate: *intone*). Deferred to a minting pass. (The Census/Crucible `C`
262	  collision once parked here is resolved: Census → Terrier.)
263	- **Cross-Quire information flow** — the graph-property hazard; the agency lattice is its
264	  clearance lattice. Belongs to the choreography effort, not here.
265	- **Identity upgrade** — confined-credential MVP → federated STS (envoy) fast-follow; rides
266	  ₣BZ landing federation.
267	- **HA** — skip for the private path.
268	- **Agentic surfaces** — a non-goal for the private Quire unless deliberately re-opened (and
269	  then governed by IAM, never the sentry).
270	- **Terminus consumer class** — §11's open question: container-only consumers (plain
271	  container networking) vs. a host-resident consumer (needs a local forward-proxy / endpoint
272	  seam from the terminus, still no host VPN). Container-only is the lean; unsettled.
273	- **SigV4 spike (cinched first move, §13)** — two `curl --aws-sigv4` calls (clean

276	## 11. Launch shape — the Outpost's local terminus
277	
278	A same-session addendum extending §5 and §7. The shaping above left one thing unsaid: the
279	Outpost is "peer-bearing" on the *AWS* side, but where does the tunnel's *local* end live?
280	Three candidate homes were weighed; two are rejected here and the third adopted as the working
281	shape.
282	
283	**Not in the sentry.** Folding the tunnel client into the sentry inverts the sentry's purpose.
284	The sentry is a *containment-harness* component — it jails a possibly-hostile bottle and exists
285	to prove the jail holds (the Ifrit/tadmor stance: the inside is the threat). A tunnel terminus
286	serving a *trusted* reach is the opposite stance — trusted infrastructure, not a contained
287	prisoner. Crossing the two makes the sentry mean two contradictory things at once. The
288	predecessor's answer was WireGuard-in-sentry (the 2026-04-15 conduit memo, §12); it is rejected
289	here.
290	
291	**Not a bottle-less crucible either.** The tempting shortcut — a crucible charged with no
292	bottle — is a category error. The crucible *is* the containment harness; a bottle-less crucible
293	is a jail with no prisoner, and hollowing out its one purpose to borrow its lifecycle plumbing
294	is abstraction strain, not a feature.
295	
296	**The factoring that holds.** The image ecosystem (vessel → hallmark → nameplate) is separable
297	from the runtime harness (crucible). The crucible is one *consumer* of hallmark images. The
298	local terminus is a **second launch shape** beside it — same build pipeline, different runtime —
299	not a variant of the first. "A whole different notion of launched container" is the correct
300	read, and the signal that it belongs beside the crucible, not inside it.
301	
302	**Its home in the vocabulary: the Outpost's local end.** The terminus is the client-side
303	counterpart of the EC2 peer — built as its own vessel, launched standalone. The Outpost now has
304	two ends (AWS peer + local terminus), both operated by `raise`/`strike`; no new verbs, no new
305	top-level concept. This finishes the migration this memo began: away from WireGuard-in-sentry,
306	toward a dedicated terminus.
307	
308	**"No host WireGuard" is a design constraint, and the terminus honors it.** The tunnel client
309	lives in a *container*; its config rides inside the hallmark through the same pipeline as
310	everything else, and the workstation host never joins a VPN. That is the "path based on the
311	container image ecosystem" — literally.
312	
313	**MVP untouched.** The terminus is fast-follow only: it exists only once there is an Outpost to
314	terminate. The MVP (allowlist reach, sentry egress-lockdown, no Outpost, no VPC) is unaffected,
315	and there the sentry keeps doing exactly what it does today. This addendum adds no MVP surface —
316	consistent with §9's rule that the forward vision must not touch the MVP.
317	
318	**One open question, named not solved (§10): how a consumer reaches the terminus.** Container
319	consumers reach it by plain container networking — trivial, never touching the host. A
320	host-resident consumer (operator tooling outside any container) would need the terminus to
321	expose a local seam — most plausibly a forward proxy / endpoint-override the SDK points at —
322	*still* no host VPN. Which consumer class is in scope changes the design; container-only is
323	dramatically simpler and is the current lean.
324	
325	## 12. Consume-side governance — Precentor, Chorister, Prebend (2026-06-10 addendum)
326	
327	A next-day addendum recording the governance role shape and its grounding research. Settled as
328	working language; the build itself remains distant.
329	
330	**The pre-authorized decision.** RBSHR's asymmetric-sovereignty stance reserves exactly this
331	slot: a parallel role hierarchy on the AWS side is "a deliberate architectural decision...
332	not an incremental drift." This is that decision arriving — consume-side (governing who may
333	invoke Quires and at what cost), not build-side. The delivery-side batch (embassy, envoy,
334	bullion, entrust, accredit, recall) keeps its diplomatic register; the consume side rides the
335	Quire's ecclesiastical register, so the two AWS efforts stay distinguishable by ear.
336	
337	**The shape.** One administering role plus N constrained principals. Unlike the GCP trio
338	(Governor/Director/Retriever divide *function*), the consume-side citizens divide
339	*people/workloads* — N instances of the same shape with different ceilings. This is the ₣BZ
340	citizen/capability model instantiated on AWS; the Quire memo's "capability reference (who may
341	invoke)" resolves to these principals.
342	
343	| Concept | Noun | Register grounding |
344	|---|---|---|
345	| Admin role — mints/retires constrained users, owns envelopes | **Precentor** | The cathedral officer who directs the quire — governs who sings and what they sing (models and conduct, not just money) |
346	| Constrained user — capped principal invoking within bounds | **Chorister** | A member of the quire, granted a place, voicing within the precentor's direction |
347	| Per-chorister envelope — model list, agency ceiling, allowances | **Prebend** | The medieval term for a fixed allowance endowed to a cathedral office-holder — spend-cap-as-endowed-allowance is the literal historical meaning |
348	
349	All three grep clean across the repo (verified 2026-06-10). The chantry→Quire→endow lineage
350	was always about governed allowances — a chantry was funded by an endowment — so the prebend
351	completes a family, not a theme.
352	
353	**Civic homogenization (elected 2026-06-10).** The ₣BZ civic structure absorbs this hierarchy
354	whole; nothing here invents role machinery:
355	
356	- **The Cloister is a polity** — "admission authority is scoped to the polity it admits into"
357	  generalizes directly; the Cloister is a polity whose enforcement plane is AWS IAM instead of
358	  GCP IAM. Its ledger file joins the **Manor-bucket Terrier collection** (per the ₣BZ paddock's
359	  reshaped Terrier: per-polity ledger files, N independent, no global bookkeeping — a new
360	  polity kind costs one file). Payor founds the Cloister and creates its ledger file; the
361	  bare tier (no GCP at all, §14) keeps a stated exception — local or Cloister-homed ledger.
362	  Cross-cloud caution, priced: Cloister *admin ceremonies* write a GCP bucket, but the Terrier
363	  is admin-plane only — working verbs (actual inference) never touch it, so a GCP outage
364	  cannot block a chorister's Bedrock calls, only enrollment ceremonies.
365	- **Civic verbs, not invest/divest.** ₣BZ retires invest/divest/mantle/roster; choristers are
366	  **enfranchised, granted, revoked, expelled** — the same verb set, second polity kind. The
367	  AWS credential analog of rekey rides whichever identity tier (§14) the Cloister runs.
368	- **The precentor follows the governor's dissolution**: keeps its quoin, dissolves
369	  structurally — a precentor is a citizen holding the precentor capability-set for its
370	  Cloister. Precentors create precentors within their own Cloister; cross-Cloister
371	  administration does not exist (account scoping already forbids it).
372	- **The prebend is the chorister's ledger entry**, honoring the code-vs-data seam:
373	  capability-set *definitions* (model-list and agency shapes, e.g. "counsel-quire access")
374	  are code, global; the chorister's *held sets plus allowance figures* are data, local to the
375	  Cloister's ledger file. This sharpens §9's intersection resolution: the citizen-side
376	  envelope is, concretely, a ledger row. Enforcement is server-side (AWS) IAM; the ledger is
377	  intent; no file is ever the boundary — the ₣BZ cinch, ported verbatim.
378	
379	**The premises: the Cloister.** The consume-side AWS account itself — where precentor,
380	choristers, prebends, Quires, and (fast-follow) the Outpost's AWS end live — is the
381	**Cloister**: the walled monastic enclosure, named for what the account *is* (the
382	blast-radius, quota, and billing boundary; all three are account-grain in AWS) and for what
383	the whole effort is *for* — cloistered conversations. Sovereign-setup sibling of the Manor's
384	depot and the delivery-side embassy: stood up from Recipe-Bottle-shipped templates, owned by
385	the operator wearing an AWS hat. (`Close`, the cathedral-precinct term, was considered and
386	rejected as a heavily overloaded software word; grep-findability is a minting criterion.)
387	Cardinality is a real design question, not decoration — the Cloister is the hardest isolation
388	boundary AWS offers, so "one per operator" vs. "one per privacy domain" shapes the build.
389	Lean: one per operator until a second privacy domain earns its own walls; deferred to the
390	heat.
391	
392	**Enforcement grounding (AWS facilities, verified 2026-06-10).** Model constraint is airtight
393	and native: IAM scopes `bedrock:InvokeModel` to explicit model/inference-profile ARNs — the
394	Quire's "governed invocation principal" is exactly this object. Agency ceilings are likewise
395	IAM (denies on the agentic API surfaces). Spend constraint has **no native hard cap**; what
396	exists is a ladder ordered by reaction time:
397	
398	1. **Application inference profiles** + cost-allocation tags — per-call attribution flowing to
399	   Cost Explorer / CUR / Budgets. One AIP per (model, chorister). Tags are **non-retroactive**:
400	   a mis-established binding loses attribution data permanently, which is the hard argument
401	   for ceremony-enforced establishment order (AIP → tag activation → budget → action).
402	2. **AWS Budgets + budget actions** — threshold breach auto-attaches an IAM deny policy.
403	   Day-grain; billing lag means a runaway day can overshoot before it trips.
404	3. **CloudWatch token metrics + alarm + Lambda deny** — Bedrock emits token counts as metrics
405	   (dimensionable per inference profile) *without* invocation logging, so minutes-grain
406	   cutoff is privacy-clean (boundary C untouched).
407	4. **Counting proxy in the data path** — the only true synchronous cap. Named non-goal: it
408	   spends "no standing software" and (via TLS origination) complicates boundary A. If it ever
409	   comes, its natural body is the §11 terminus growing a meter.
410	
411	**Cap vocabulary, kept honest.** A prebend declares `soft` (notify) and `firm` (auto-deny,
412	bounded overshoot — rungs 2/3). `hard` (synchronous wall) is reserved for the proxy tier and
413	does not exist in the MVP. Same discipline as `agency: none` — the guarantee is a named,
414	enforced property, never a hopeful adjective.
415	
416	**Why this layer is load-bearing (anti-crap-wrap).** AWS supplies every primitive and composes
417	none of them: there is no native object uniting principal + AIP set + tags + budget + action
418	wiring + agency clause. The layer is ceremony codification, not runtime interposition — the
419	same move as the GCP role hierarchy, pointed at a second cloud. Its teeth: establishment-order
420	guarantees (the non-retroactive-tag hazard above), roster, and the declared-vs-actual drift
421	audit (the ₣BZ intent-vs-enforcement spine; the prebend is the declared side of the diff).
422	
423	**No resident program.** Establishment (invest/divest/roster/endow) is control-plane REST;
424	the enforcement reactors are AWS-resident and event-driven (Budgets actions, alarm-triggered
425	Lambdas shipped as templates — "stood up from Recipe-Bottle-shipped templates," per §1). The
426	genuine implementation strain is **SigV4**: AWS signs per-request (canonical request + chained
427	HMAC derivation), versus the GCP side's single JWT exchange. §13 decomposes that strain and
428	finds the probable resolution inside curl itself — no signing helper, no daemon; the
429	Sentry/Outpost-analog resident process belongs only to ladder rung 4, which is deferred.
430	
431	**Menu framing settled.** The prebend *is* a per-chorister menu — model list as approved
432	options, agency as ceiling, allowance as cap — the citizen-side envelope of §9's
433	intersection resolution. Recipe Bottle governs both envelopes; the chorister picks within
434	their intersection.
435	
436	## 13. AWS transport — SigV4 in bash, and the curl seam (2026-06-10 addendum)
437	
438	Same-day companion to §12, recording why "bash for AWS auth" is harder than the GCP precedent,
439	where the difficulty actually lives, and the seam that probably dissolves most of it. Nothing
440	here is built; the spike below is **cinched as the first move** before any transport code.
441	
442	**The GCP baseline (evidence: `rbgo_OAuth.sh`).** Google's JWT-bearer flow front-loads all
443	crypto into one per-session mint: sign a ~200-byte self-contained document once, exchange it,
444	then every subsequent REST call carries a bare `Authorization: Bearer` header — zero crypto
445	per request. That decoupling is what makes the BCG failure-capture ceremony affordable (one
446	module, one retry envelope, structured JSON error bodies to discriminate on).
447	
448	**SigV4 inverts the seam — four distinct strains:**
449	
450	1. **Cardinality** — every request is signed, so every REST helper and every poll iteration
451	   becomes crypto-bearing; the ceremony owed once per session is owed per call.
452	2. **Binary intermediates** — the signing key derives through four chained HMAC-SHA256 steps
453	   whose intermediates are binary; bash variables cannot hold NUL bytes, so each step
454	   round-trips as hex through an openssl subprocess (`-macopt hexkey:`) — workable, ~5× the
455	   JWT path's ceremony, per request.
456	3. **Canonicalization byte-exactness** — URI-encoding with AWS's specific rules, codepoint-
457	   sorted query params, folded headers; bash has no native URI-encoder, and the failure mode
458	   is a remote opaque 403 `SignatureDoesNotMatch`, not a loud local error — the exact failure
459	   shape BCG discipline exists to kill.
460	4. **Two sources of truth** — you sign a *reconstruction* of the request while curl builds the
461	   wire bytes and mutates headers on its own (`Host`, `Content-Length`, `Expect:`); any
462	   divergence breaks the signature. Interface Contamination with a foreign tool inside the
463	   crypto loop, on every call.
464	
465	**The curl seam.** curl signs SigV4 natively: `--aws-sigv4 "aws:amz:«region»:«service»"` with
466	`--user "«key»:«secret»"`; STS temporary credentials ride a plain `x-amz-security-token`
467	header. Because the tool that builds the wire bytes also signs them, strains 2–4 dissolve at a
468	stroke and bash never touches an HMAC. Costs, named honestly:
469	
470	- **A curl version floor** (feature landed 7.75, matured through 8.x) — a new pin to state in
471	  the dependency inventory, where curl already sits as a consumer dependency.
472	- **A Palisade dependence**: curl's signer has a surveyed bug history (wildcard paths,
473	  empty-value query params, a missing `x-amz-content-sha256` quirk against some services).
474	  We cannot edit curl — conduct is characterize/contain/absorb the surveyed signatures, per
475	  the ROE Palisade discipline, with the signing flags composed in exactly one module function.
476	- **Trust-surface honesty**: curl is already trusted for every TLS byte RB moves, so this is
477	  not a new *kind* of trust — but `--aws-sigv4` is a far less-trodden path within that trusted
478	  neighbor than its TLS core, and trust transfers imperfectly across surfaces. Hence the spike.
479	
480	**The one RB-relevant risk shape**: Bedrock *runtime* paths embed ARNs in the URI
481	(`/model/arn%3Aaws%3A…/converse` when invoking via inference profile) — colons-in-path is
482	precisely where signing implementations historically disagree. Establishment ceremony (IAM,
483	Budgets, Bedrock control plane) uses clean paths; only the one-shot Quire probe touches the
484	runtime shape, so the exposure is confined to a single verb.
485	
486	**Cinched: spike before build.** Two calls from the workstation retire most of the
487	uncertainty: (1) a clean control-plane call (e.g. IAM `GetUser` or Bedrock
488	`ListFoundationModels`); (2) the probe-shaped `Converse` call with an ARN-bearing path. If
489	both land, bash keeps sovereignty over AWS transport at the cost of a version floor and one
490	membrane. Hand-rolled bash SigV4 (hex-chained HMACs, testable against AWS's published SigV4
491	test-vector suite) is the **named fallback, not built** — reached for only if curl's signer
492	mishandles a request shape that cannot be reshaped.
493	
494	**Why there is no GCP analog — the seam principle.** Crypto belongs in the transport tool
495	exactly when it is *transport-coupled* (a signature over the wire bytes only the
496	request-builder can compute); it belongs at openssl when it is *document-coupled* (a
497	self-contained signed artifact like a JWT). Google chose a document-coupled scheme, so
498	`rbgo_OAuth.sh` already sits at the minimal seam for it — curl offers nothing that would
499	simplify the existing GCP path (`--oauth2-bearer` is cosmetic sugar over `-H`, and curl's
500	native `--retry` cannot replace the rbgo/rbuh retry loops, whose discrimination reads
501	response *bodies* — `invalid_grant` shapes, SA-propagation races — that curl cannot see;
502	that complexity is load-bearing). The two clouds get different seams because they chose
503	different coupling, and matching the seam to the coupling is the whole lesson.
504	
505	## 14. Standalone posture — the deployment ladder (2026-06-10 addendum)
506	
507	Settled in the same session as §12/§13: the Bedrock consume side **can stand alone without a
508	Depot**, and the original counterpoints resolved into rung descriptions rather than
509	objections. The structural key: federation canon **D1** already establishes two permanent
510	identity tiers (keyfile: free, no-org; federation: paid, org-required) selected by a mode
511	enum — the Cloister's identity story is *the same enum instantiated across a cloud boundary*,
512	not a new model. And the federation issuer machinery is **org/Manor-side, not depot-side**
513	(payor-establish builds Manor + org; depot-levy is separate and skippable), so the middle
514	rung exists.
515	
516	| Tier | GCP footprint | Identity | Image provenance | Chorister roster |
517	|---|---|---|---|---|
518	| **Bare Cloister** | none | resting confined credential | kludge-grade, said out loud | local/Cloister-homed (stated Terrier exception) |
519	| **Manor + Cloister** | Manor + org, no Depot | federated, no resting AWS keys | kludge unless images arrive from a Directored ecosystem | ledger file in the Manor-bucket Terrier |
520	| **Manor + Depot + Cloister** | full | federated | Director-ordained, vouched, chorister-summoned | ledger file in the Manor-bucket Terrier |
521	
522	- **Provenance (the chorister-as-retriever answer).** Choristers consume images the way
523	  retrievers do — a Director somewhere ordained and vouched them; the chorister summons,
524	  never builds. Provenance is preserved with zero foundry footprint per user. Residuals,
525	  named: a retriever-style pull carries a GCP read credential (or the org distributes images
526	  by another channel), and the bare rung — no Director anywhere — accepts kludge-grade
527	  provenance as its honest label, paralleling D1's free tier accepting resting keys.
528	- **Legibility before tunnel.** "Users will appreciate the visible WireGuard container" is a
529	  real fourth force on the Outpost beyond §7's three triggers — visible security earns trust
530	  invisible iptables don't — but it buys *legibility*, not a privacy boundary the MVP lacks.
531	  Sequencing: make the allowlist visible first (scry, traffic logs, a "here is everywhere
532	  your bytes can go" report); build the Outpost when a §7 trigger fires or demand survives
533	  the legibility fix.
534	- **Decoupling invariant (cheap now, expensive to retrofit).** Standalone-capable is
535	  checkable architecture: the Quire/Cloister kindling chain must never source a GCP regime.
536	  The middle and full tiers touch GCP only in admin-plane ceremonies (ledger writes,
537	  federation mint), never in the working-verb path.
538

548	  Terrier homing: the Terrier is per-polity ledger files in a **Manor bucket** (payor creates
549	  at levy; governor-writeable own-polity-only; admin-plane reads; working verbs never touch
550	  it); civic verbs (enfranchise/expel/grant/revoke/rekey) replace invest/divest/mantle/roster;
551	  governor dissolves to citizen + capability-set. §12's civic homogenization and §14's middle
552	  tier ride these cinches.
553	- curl/AWS, verified 2026-06-10 (§13 transport seam): curl native `--aws-sigv4` signing with
554	  session-token header support; surveyed signer bugs (curl issues #10129 empty query params,
555	  #11007 x-amz-content-sha256, wildcard-path mis-signing); AWS SigV4 troubleshooting docs and
556	  published test-vector suite for any hand-rolled fallback.
557	- AWS, verified 2026-06-10 (§12 enforcement ladder): IAM model/inference-profile ARN scoping
558	  for `bedrock:InvokeModel`; application inference profiles with cost-allocation tags
559	  (non-retroactive, ~24h activation lag); AWS Budgets actions auto-applying IAM deny policies;
560	  Bedrock CloudWatch token-count metrics independent of invocation logging; no native hard
561	  spend cap (AWS's own guidance: synchronous enforcement requires a gateway/proxy); TPM service
562	  quotas are account-grain and self-serve adjustable upward only.
563	- AWS, verified 2026-06-09: Bedrock is serverless/stateless and ZDR-by-default
564	  (data-protection docs); provider isolation via dedicated deployment accounts; model
565	  invocation logging opt-in/off; abuse detection automated/no-human-review; Claude on Bedrock
566	  under Commercial Terms (no training); cross-region inference residency (geographic vs
567	  global); plain inference has no network reach while AgentCore Browser/Code-Interpreter and
568	  Lambda action groups execute server-side and can reach the internet; pricing — Sonnet
569	  $3/$15, Opus $5/$25 per M tokens, PrivateLink endpoint ~$0.01/hr/AZ, t3.nano ~$3.80/mo,
570	  public IPv4 ~$3.65/mo, NAT gateway ~$0.045/hr.
